title: C Linked Lists
content:
### Linked Lists in C

A linked list is a dynamic data structure where elements (nodes) are connected via pointers.

### Node Structure

```c
struct Node {
  int data;
  struct Node *next;
};
```

### Types of Linked Lists

| Type | Description |
|------|-------------|
| Singly linked | Each node points to next node; last points to NULL |
| Doubly linked | Each node has prev and next pointers |
| Circular | Last node points back to first |

### Basic Operations

| Operation | Description | Complexity |
|-----------|-------------|------------|
| Insert at beginning | Create node, link to head | O(1) |
| Insert at end | Traverse to end, link new node | O(n) |
| Delete a node | Unlink and free node | O(n) |
| Search | Traverse until found | O(n) |
| Traverse | Visit all nodes | O(n) |

### Advantages vs Arrays

| Feature | Linked List | Array |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| Insert/delete at start | O(1) | O(n) |
| Random access | O(n) (must traverse) | O(1) (by index) |
| Memory | Dynamic, per-node allocation | Fixed block |
| Cache locality | Poor | Excellent |

---

> **Key Point:** Linked lists excel at frequent insertions/deletions. Arrays are better for frequent random access. Always free deleted nodes to prevent memory leaks.

quiz.question: What is the time complexity of inserting at the beginning of a linked list?
